title: OpenAI Launches GPT-Realtime-2 and Two New Voice API Models
summary: OpenAI released three new real-time audio models — GPT-Realtime-2, GPT-Realtime-Translate, and GPT-Realtime-Whisper — bringing GPT-5-class reasoning to live voice interactions.
content: On May 7, 2026, OpenAI introduced GPT-Realtime-2, its first voice model powered by GPT-5-class reasoning, alongside two companion models: GPT-Realtime-Translate and GPT-Realtime-Whisper. GPT-Realtime-2 expands the context window from 32K to 128K tokens and handles full audio-in, audio-out processing — reasoning happens inside the audio loop rather than between transcription and synthesis steps — enabling it to call tools, handle interruptions, and carry multi-step conversations naturally. GPT-Realtime-Translate supports live speech translation across 70+ input languages into 13 output languages, while GPT-Realtime-Whisper delivers low-latency streaming speech-to-text for captions and meeting notes. On OpenAI's benchmarks, GPT-Realtime-2 scored 15.2% higher than its predecessor on Big Bench Audio and 13.8% higher on Audio MultiChallenge; early customers like Zillow reported a 26-point lift in call-success rate. The launch ends the Realtime API's beta phase and positions a single-model stack as a direct alternative to multi-vendor voice pipelines built on Whisper, ElevenLabs, and separate reasoning models.

title: Anthropic Introduces "Dreaming" — AI Agents That Learn From Their Own Past Sessions
summary: Anthropic unveiled "dreaming," a capability that lets Claude agents review their past sessions, extract patterns, and self-improve over time — a step toward production-ready autonomous agents.
content: At its second annual Code with Claude developer conference on May 6, 2026, Anthropic announced three updates to its Claude Managed Agents platform, the most novel being "dreaming" — a scheduled process that reviews an agent's past sessions and memory stores, surfaces recurring mistakes and successful patterns, and curates memories so agents improve continuously across deployments. Unlike the session-level memory Anthropic launched earlier this year, dreaming works at a higher abstraction level, identifying insights no single agent session could surface alone, including shared preferences across teams of agents and workflows that multiple agents converge on independently. Alongside dreaming (available in research preview), Anthropic moved two previously experimental features — outcomes-based evaluation and multi-agent orchestration — into public beta. The company said teams using its Managed Agents platform have shipped 10x faster than those building custom agent infrastructure from scratch, framing the platform as an operating system for AI development rather than a raw API.

title: EU Clinches Deal to Roll Back AI Act Restrictions by More Than a Year
summary: EU legislators agreed to delay high-risk AI rules until December 2027 and exempt industrial AI applications, marking the bloc's first significant rollback of digital-space regulations.
content: Following overnight negotiations, the European Parliament and EU member states reached a deal on May 7, 2026 to delay the enforcement of high-risk AI restrictions under the EU AI Act from August 2026 to December 2027 — a postponement of over a year. The agreement also largely exempts industrial AI applications from the law's scope, a major concession won by Germany after Chancellor Friedrich Merz pressed for the change to protect competitiveness of companies like Siemens and Bosch; those firms will only need to comply with existing machinery regulations rather than the AI Act. Medical devices were not exempted and remain covered. The deal does add new prohibitions, including a ban on AI systems generating sexualized deepfakes of identifiable people and a ban on AI-generated child pornography. Commission President Ursula von der Leyen called it "a simple, innovation-friendly environment" for AI in Europe. The rollback signals a broader shift in EU tech regulation under competitive pressure from the United States, as the bloc recalibrates between citizen protections and the need to remain competitive in the global AI race.

title: US Government Agency to Safety-Test Frontier AI Models Before Release
summary: The Center for AI Standards and Innovation signed pre-deployment testing agreements with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI, as the White House considers an executive order creating a formal AI vetting system.
content: The Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI), a NIST division within the US Department of Commerce, announced on May 6, 2026 that it has signed agreements with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI to conduct pre-deployment evaluations of frontier AI models before public release — joining Anthropic and OpenAI, which signed similar agreements two years ago under the Biden administration. The agreements give CAISI access to conduct safety evaluations and targeted research to better assess frontier AI capabilities and advance AI security standards. The announcements closely followed Bloomberg reports that the White House is preparing an executive order to create a formal vetting system for all new AI models, prompted in part by concerns over Anthropic's Mythos model, which demonstrated the ability to find and exploit network vulnerabilities. Analysts called the moves a significant shift in policy direction from an administration that had previously taken a more permissive, laissez-faire stance toward AI regulation, adding that concerns about agentic AI's autonomous behaviors are pushing governments toward security-by-design frameworks before models reach the public.

title: AWS Launches Agent Toolkit: 40+ Skills and a Managed MCP Server for AI Coding Agents
summary: AWS released the Agent Toolkit for AWS, a production-ready suite of over 40 agent skills, a generally available MCP server, and pre-built plugins to help AI coding agents work reliably on AWS infrastructure.
content: On May 6, 2026, AWS launched the Agent Toolkit for AWS, a free, production-ready platform designed to help AI coding agents build and operate on AWS with fewer errors, lower token costs, and enterprise-grade governance. The toolkit includes more than 40 curated "skills" — validated, up-to-date procedures covering infrastructure-as-code, storage, analytics, serverless, containers, and AI services — that agents load on demand rather than improvising from potentially outdated training knowledge. The AWS MCP Server, now generally available, gives coding agents secure, auditable access to any AWS service via the Model Context Protocol, with IAM-based guardrails, CloudWatch metrics, CloudTrail logging, and sandboxed Python execution. Three ready-made plugins bundle the server and relevant skills: AWS Core for full-stack app development, AWS Data Analytics for data pipelines, and AWS Agents for building production agents with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore. The toolkit is available at no additional charge — customers pay only for the AWS resources their agents consume — and represents AWS's strategic push to make its cloud the default runtime environment for agentic AI workloads.

title: IBM Study: 76% of Organizations Now Have a Chief AI Officer, Up From 26% a Year Ago
summary: A global IBM survey of 2,000 CEOs finds that enterprises are rapidly restructuring leadership around AI, with nearly two-thirds of CEOs now comfortable making major strategic decisions based on AI-generated input.
content: IBM's 2026 CEO study, surveying 2,000 senior leaders across 33 geographies, found that 76% of organizations now have a Chief AI Officer — up from just 26% in 2025 — as CEOs redesign C-suite roles to embed AI into core business operations rather than treating it as a separate technology initiative. Nearly two-thirds of CEOs (64%) say they are comfortable making major strategic decisions informed by AI-generated input, and 83% agree that AI sovereignty is now essential to business strategy. By 2030, surveyed CEOs expect that 48% of operational decisions with codifiable guardrails will be made by AI without human intervention, up from 25% today. Despite high ambitions, only 25% of the workforce currently uses AI regularly on the job, and organizations that redesigned five core business areas — technology, finance, HR, operations, and cross-functional collaboration — were four times more likely to have delivered on their AI business objectives. The findings reinforce that AI's most transformative impact is coming through organizational redesign rather than model adoption alone, with talent reskilling and governance frameworks emerging as the critical differentiators.
